text: This paper examines the dynamics of global student mobility and the decline of the US in the international student market. While the US is still the number one country in terms of the total number of international students hosted, its global market share is decreasing while that of its competitors such as France, Germany, Japan and Australia is increasing at a much faster rate. The paper describes how the major countries are undertaking policy measures to try and attract students including easing immigration and work permit restrictions, increasing the amount of instruction in the English language in non-English speaking countries and by trying to position regions such as Singapore, Malaysia and Panama as major international education hubs. The paper also examines capacity building within the traditional sending countries such as China and India. These countries have a growing capacity to compete with other countries for international students because they are increasingly able to provide a high quality education to students who choose not to go abroad. Our findings suggest that the US is no longer the automatic leader in terms of international student recruitment due to a rise in global competition and the increased diversity of the international student mobility market.
